seo.imageAlt: A bowl of rich, dark authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) served with chicken and rice.
seo.imageCaption: Experience the deep, complex flavors of Oaxaca with this traditional Black Mole (Mole Negro) recipe.

description: Oaxacan Black Mole, or Mole Negro, is a legendary sauce from Oaxaca, Mexico, known for its incredible depth of flavor. This traditional recipe combines numerous chiles, spices, nuts, seeds, and Mexican chocolate to create a rich, dark, and complex culinary masterpiece. It's a labor of love that results in an unforgettable dining experience, often served with poultry or pork, embodying the heart of Mexican cuisine.

ingredients.notes: about 3 oz, like Ibarra or Taza

instructions.title: Prepare and Toast Chiles
instructions.text: Carefully remove stems and seeds from all dried chiles. Heat a comal or dry skillet over medium-low heat. Toast the chiles one by one, pressing lightly with tongs, for about 15-30 seconds per side until fragrant but not burnt. Toasting too long will make them bitter. Immediately transfer toasted chiles to a bowl and cover with hot water for 20-30 minutes to rehydrate. Reserve the soaking water.

instructions.title: Roast Aromatics and Toast Spices
instructions.text: While chiles rehydrate, roast tomatoes and tomatillos on a comal until softened and lightly charred. In a separate dry skillet, toast almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds until golden and fragrant. Set aside. Toast the cinnamon stick, cloves, peppercorns, and anise seeds for 1-2 minutes until aromatic, then set aside. Lightly toast the oregano and thyme for 30 seconds.

instructions.title: Fry Remaining Ingredients
instructions.text: Heat 1/4 cup of lard or oil in a large pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Fry the chopped onion and garlic until translucent, about 5-7 minutes. Add the sliced plantain and fry until golden, about 3-5 minutes. Add the raisins and fry for about 1 minute until plump. Remove all fried ingredients with a slotted spoon and set aside, reserving the lard/oil in the pot.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Paste
instructions.text: Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving the soaking water. In a blender, combine the rehydrated chiles, roasted tomatoes, tomatillos, fried onion, garlic, plantain, raisins, all toasted nuts, seeds, and spices (cinnamon, cloves, peppercorns, anise, oregano, thyme). Add 1-2 cups of chicken broth and blend until completely smooth, adding more chile soaking water or broth if needed to achieve a thick, pourable paste. This may require blending in batches.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: Heat the remaining 1/4 cup of lard or oil in the same large pot over medium heat. Carefully pour the blended mole paste into the hot lard/oil. Stir constantly to prevent sticking and spattering. Cook for at least 20-30 minutes, stirring frequently, until the mole darkens in color and thickens considerably. This step is crucial for developing flavor.

instructions.title: Simmer and Finish
instructions.text: Gradually stir in the remaining chicken broth into the cooked mole paste. Bring to a gentle simmer. Add the Mexican chocolate and sugar. Stir until the chocolate is fully melted and incorporated. Continue to simmer the mole for at least 1 hour, or up to 2 hours, stirring occasionally, allowing the flavors to meld and deepen. Season with salt to taste. The mole should be rich, dark, and velvety. If it becomes too thick, add a little more broth or water.

tips: To achieve the deepest color and flavor, ensure your chiles are properly toasted but never burnt. A dark, rich color is key for Mole Negro.
Do not skip the long simmering time. Mole flavors deepen and meld significantly with extended cooking, transforming from individual ingredients into a harmonious sauce.
Mole Negro can be made ahead of time. In fact, it often tastes even better the next day! Store it in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 5 days or freeze for up to 3 months.
article:
Unlocking the secrets of Mexican cuisine often leads to discovery, and few dishes capture the imagination quite like Mole Negro. This iconic sauce, hailing from the vibrant state of Oaxaca, is more than just a meal; it's a culinary journey, a symphony of flavors, and a testament to the rich history of Mexican gastronomy. If you've ever dreamt of recreating the magic of authentic Mexican dinner recipes in your own kitchen, diving into the world of Oaxacan Black Mole is an unforgettable experience. Prepare yourself for a labor of love that rewards you with one of the most complex, profound, and utterly delicious sauces known to humankind.

## What is Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro)?

Oaxacan Black Mole, or Mole Negro, is widely considered the "king" of moles. Originating from Oaxaca, a region renowned for its seven distinct moles, Mole Negro stands out for its deep, almost ebony color and an incredibly intricate flavor profile. It's a sauce that defies simple description, weaving together smoky, sweet, spicy, earthy, and savory notes into a harmonious whole.

The complexity of Mole Negro comes from its extensive list of ingredients, often numbering 20 to 30 or even more. Key components typically include several types of dried chiles (such as mulato, pasilla, and ancho), nuts and seeds (almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, pepitas), spices (cinnamon, cloves, cumin, black pepper), fruits (plantain, raisins), vegetables (tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, garlic), and a touch of Mexican chocolate and piloncillo (unrefined cane sugar) to add depth and balance. The characteristic dark color isn't just from the chiles; many ingredients are roasted, toasted, and even charred slightly to achieve that signature deep hue and smoky essence. This isn't a sauce you whip up in minutes; it's a multi-day process for some, a ritual that culminates in a truly spectacular dish, often reserved for special occasions like weddings, fiestas, and holidays.

## Ingredients You Need for Oaxacan Black Mole

Making authentic Oaxacan Black Mole requires a commitment to sourcing a variety of ingredients. While the list might seem daunting, each component plays a vital role in building the sauce's layered flavor.

**Dried Chiles (essential for color and flavor):**
* 6-8 Mulato chiles
* 6-8 Pasilla Oaxaca chiles (or Pasilla Mexicano if Oaxacan are unavailable)
* 4-6 Ancho chiles
* 2-3 Chipotle or Morita chiles

**Aromatics & Vegetables:**
* 1 large white onion, roughly chopped
* 6-8 cloves garlic, peeled
* 2-3 ripe Roma tomatoes, roughly chopped
* 3-4 tomatillos, husked and quartered
* 1 ripe plantain, peeled and sliced (or a very ripe banana)

**Nuts & Seeds (for thickening and richness):**
* 1/2 cup raw almonds
* 1/4 cup raw peanuts
* 1/4 cup pepitas (pumpkin seeds)
* 2 tablespoons sesame seeds

**Spices (toast lightly for best flavor):**
* 1 teaspoon whole cumin seeds
* 1 teaspoon whole coriander seeds
* 1/2 teaspoon whole black peppercorns
* 4-5 whole cloves
* 1 small stick Mexican cinnamon (about 2 inches)
* 1/2 teaspoon anise seeds (optional)

**Sweeteners & Thickeners:**
* 1 tablet (90g) Mexican chocolate (e.g., Ibarra or Abuelita)
* 1-2 ounces piloncillo (or 2-3 tablespoons dark brown sugar)
* 1/4 cup raisins or prunes
* 2-3 stale corn tortillas or 1 slice stale bread

**Liquids & Fats:**
* 6-8 cups chicken broth (or vegetable broth for a vegetarian version)
* 1/4 cup lard or vegetable oil
* Salt to taste

## Step-by-Step Instructions to Make Mole Negro

Patience is key to making a truly magnificent Mole Negro. Break down the process into manageable steps:

1.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Carefully toast each chile on a dry comal or skillet over medium heat for 10-20 seconds per side, until fragrant and slightly puffed. Be careful not to burn them, as this will make the mole bitter. Remove stems and seeds (reserve some seeds for toasting if desired for extra heat). Place toasted chiles in a bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until soft.

2.  **Toast Nuts & Seeds:** In a clean, dry skillet, toast almonds, peanuts, pepitas, and sesame seeds separately until lightly golden and fragrant. Set aside.

3.  **Toast Spices:** Lightly toast cumin, coriander, peppercorns, cloves, cinnamon, and anise seeds (if using) in the same skillet until aromatic. Be careful not to burn. Set aside.

4.  **Fry Aromatics, Fruits & Vegetables:** Heat lard or oil in a large pot or skillet. Fry the chopped onion and garlic until softened and lightly browned. Add tomatoes, tomatillos, and plantain slices, frying until slightly caramelized. Remove and set aside.

5.  **Fry Tortillas/Bread:** Lightly fry the stale tortillas or bread in the remaining oil until crisp. This helps thicken the mole.

6.  **Blend the Ingredients (in batches):** Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving the soaking liquid. In a blender, combine a portion of the chiles with some toasted nuts, seeds, spices, fried aromatics, vegetables, plantain, tortillas/bread, raisins, and a splash of chicken broth. Blend until very smooth. You might need to add more broth or chile soaking liquid to help it blend. Repeat until all ingredients are blended into a thick, smooth paste. For an extra smooth mole, you can pass the blended mixture through a fine-mesh sieve, though many home cooks skip this step.

7.  **Sauté the Mole Paste:** Heat a generous amount of lard or oil in a very large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour in the blended mole paste. This step is crucial for deepening the flavors. Stir constantly to prevent sticking and burning. The mole will sputter and thicken, and its color will deepen. Sauté for at least 20-30 minutes, or even longer, until the raw chile flavor is cooked out and the mole has a rich, glossy appearance.

8.  **Simmer and Season:** Gradually add chicken broth, stirring constantly, until the mole reaches your desired consistency (it should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon). Add the Mexican chocolate and piloncillo (or brown sugar), stirring until melted and fully incorporated. Season generously with salt.

9.  **Cook Low and Slow:** Reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer the mole for at least 1-2 hours, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking. The longer it simmers, the more the flavors will meld and deepen. Add more broth if it becomes too thick.

## Pro Tips for the Best Oaxacan Black Mole

*   **Quality Ingredients Matter:** The nuanced flavors of Oaxacan Black Mole depend heavily on the quality of your chiles and spices. Seek out fresh, pliable dried chiles.
*   **Don't Burn the Chiles:** Toasting them is essential for flavor, but burning them will introduce bitterness that's hard to correct.
*   **Patience is a Virtue:** Mole is not a quick dish. Give yourself ample time for each step, especially the blending and simmering. The long simmer allows the hundreds of flavors to marry beautifully.
*   **Blend Until Silky Smooth:** A powerful blender is your friend here. The smoother the paste, the more luxurious the final sauce. Strain if necessary.
*   **The Sauté is Critical:** Don't skip or rush the sautéing of the mole paste. This step "cooks" the raw chile flavor and develops incredible depth.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** Mole is about balance. Continuously taste and adjust for salt, sweetness, and heat. A little more piloncillo or chocolate can balance bitterness, while a pinch of salt can brighten all the flavors.
*   **Make Ahead:** Mole tastes even better the next day, after the flavors have had more time to meld. It's an excellent make-ahead dish for entertaining.
*   **Freezing:** Mole Negro freezes beautifully for up to 3-4 months, making it perfect for batch cooking.

## Serving Suggestions for Mole Negro

Oaxacan Black Mole is incredibly versatile and traditionally served as the star of the plate.

*   **Classic Chicken Mole:** The most common pairing is with chicken. Simmer bone-in chicken pieces directly in the mole, or shred cooked chicken and toss it with the sauce. Serve with warm corn tortillas and fluffy white rice to soak up every drop.
*   **Enchiladas de Mole:** Fill corn tortillas with shredded chicken, roll them, cover generously with warm Oaxacan Black Mole, and sprinkle with sesame seeds, crumbled queso fresco, and a drizzle of Mexican crema.
*   **Tamales:** Mole is a fantastic filling for tamales, offering a rich and savory surprise inside the masa.
*   **Vegetarian Options:** Mole Negro pairs wonderfully with roasted vegetables like sweet potatoes, cauliflower, or mushrooms. You can also use it to make vegetarian enchiladas or simply serve it over rice and beans.
*   **Creative Dishes:** Consider using it in tacos, quesadillas, or even over fried eggs for a decadent breakfast.

## Variations and Substitutions for Oaxacan Black Mole

While the classic Oaxacan Black Mole recipe is sacred to many, home cooks often make small adjustments based on preference or availability.

*   **Chile Substitutions:** If Pasilla Oaxaca chiles are hard to find, a combination of Pasilla Mexicano and a small amount of chipotle can approximate the smokiness. For more heat, add a few extra chipotles or a pinch of arbol chile powder.
*   **Nuts and Seeds:** Adjust the ratio of nuts and seeds based on what you have. Pecans, walnuts, or even sunflower seeds can be used, though they will alter the flavor slightly.
*   **Sweeteners:** Dark brown sugar can be used in place of piloncillo. Adjust the amount to taste.
*   **Broth:** For a vegetarian or vegan version, simply use high-quality vegetable broth.
*   **Fat:** While lard offers a traditional richness, vegetable oil is a perfectly acceptable substitute.
*   **Chocolate:** If Mexican chocolate is unavailable, use unsweetened dark chocolate (70% cacao or higher) and increase the piloncillo or brown sugar slightly to compensate for the lack of sweetness.
*   **Additions:** Some recipes include plantain leaves for wrapping or avocado leaves for an extra layer of flavor during simmering.

Embarking on the journey to make Oaxacan Black Mole is a culinary adventure that promises immense satisfaction. It's an opportunity to connect with a deeply rooted tradition and to create a dish that is truly a masterpiece of Mexican cuisine. While it demands time and effort, the reward – a pot of rich, complex, and utterly unforgettable Mole Negro – is well worth every step. So gather your ingredients, clear your schedule, and prepare to be amazed by the depth of flavor you can create in your own kitchen.
